01_initial_request.input_data: Persian lime transcriptome data and leaf samples from asymptomatic and HLB-symptomatic plants
01_initial_request.user_intent: To determine if the molecular mechanism of HLB tolerance in Persian lime involves the high expression of CDR genes similar to Poncirus trifoliata
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Identification of CDR gene sequences and quantification of their relative expression levels in HLB-symptomatic versus asymptomatic conditions

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Persian lime transcriptome data was available from previous analysis. [Gap] Unknown which transcripts contain Constitutive Disease Resistance (CDR) gene family members with Aspartic Protease Domains. [Decision] To achieve CDR gene identification, chose to use HMMER v3.3.2 to perform domain search on Persian lime transcriptome against Pfam database, expecting to obtain sequences containing Aspartic Protease Domains.

parameters.domain_search_criteria: one or two Aspartic Protease Domains (DTGS or DSGT)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: A total of 146 sequences from the Persian lime transcriptome showed the aspartyl protease domain

references.relevance: HMMER software for profile HMM searches

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] 146 CDR-like sequences were identified from Persian lime transcriptome. [Gap] Need to align sequences with CDR genes from P. trifoliata and C. sinensis for phylogenetic reconstruction. [Decision] To achieve sequence alignment, chose to use BioEdit Software with Muscle algorithm to perform multiple alignment on CDR sequences, expecting to obtain aligned sequences for phylogenetic analysis.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: CDR sequences from C. latifolia, P. trifoliata, and C. sinensis were aligned for phylogenetic analysis

references.relevance: BioEdit sequence alignment editor and analysis program

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] CDR sequences from three citrus species were aligned. [Gap] Phylogenetic relationships among CDR genes need to be reconstructed. [Decision] To achieve phylogenetic reconstruction, chose to use T.N.T. Software to perform Maximum Parsimony analysis with bootstrap method, expecting to obtain phylogenetic tree showing relationships among CDR genes.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Five most parsimonious trees (L=789) were recovered, and a strict consensus tree was constructed resulting in seven clades

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] CDR gene sequences were identified and phylogenetically analyzed. [Gap] Need to translate nucleotide sequences to protein for similarity analysis. [Decision] To achieve protein sequence analysis, chose to use ExPASy online tool to translate genes to protein and select largest ORF, expecting to obtain protein sequences for further analysis.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: All genes recovered from the phylogenetic tree were translated to protein for similarity analysis

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] CDR protein sequences were obtained from translation. [Gap] Need to align protein sequences for similarity comparison among species. [Decision] To achieve protein alignment, chose to use BioEdit Software with ClustalW algorithm to construct alignment, expecting to obtain aligned protein sequences for similarity matrix calculation.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Protein alignment showed highly conserved regions, particularly related to functional aspartyl protease domains

references.relevance: BioEdit sequence alignment editor and analysis program

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] CDR protein sequences were aligned. [Gap] Need to calculate genetic similarity among proteins from different species. [Decision] To achieve similarity quantification, chose to use MEGA V.11 Software to calculate genetic identity matrix based on p-distance, expecting to obtain similarity values among CDR proteins.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Similarity matrix showed some transcripts from Persian lime encode proteins very similar to homologs of P. trifoliata or C. sinensis with similarity values over 90%

references.relevance: MEGA11 Molecular Evolutionary Genetics Analysis software

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Genetic identity matrix was calculated among CDR proteins. [Gap] Need to visualize phylogenetic relationships based on protein distances. [Decision] To achieve relationship visualization, chose to use MEGA V.11 Software to construct UPGMA dendrogram, expecting to obtain dendrogram corroborating similarity values.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: UPGMA analysis corroborated the obtained similarity values from the identity matrix

references.relevance: MEGA11 Molecular Evolutionary Genetics Analysis software

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] CDR protein sequences and similarity relationships were established. [Gap] Need to analyze conserved motif structures among CDR proteins from different species. [Decision] To achieve motif analysis, chose to use MEME Software to analyze conserved motif structures, expecting to obtain motif organization patterns among ClCDR, CsCDR, and PtCDR proteins.

parameters.analysis_type: Multiple Expectation Maximization for Motif Elicitation

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Phylogenetic homologous proteins showed very similar motifs and organization; some ClCDR genes might be specific to C. latifolia based on different motif patterns

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] CDR genes were identified in Persian lime transcriptome. [Gap] Need to determine expression patterns of ClCDR genes during HLB infection. [Decision] To achieve expression analysis, chose to consult DEG database to obtain read counts, Log2 Fold change, and p-values of CDR transcripts, expecting to obtain expression data for HLB-symptomatic versus asymptomatic conditions.

parameters.data_types: reads counts, Log2 Fold change, p-values

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Majority of ClCDR gene transcripts had low counts, Log2 fold change values <2, and p-value >0.05, indicating no differences in expression during HLB disease

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Expression data from transcriptome suggested low ClCDR gene expression. [Gap] Need to validate expression patterns with independent experimental method. [Decision] To achieve RNA isolation for validation, chose to use Trizol Reagent to extract total RNA from Persian lime leaves, expecting to obtain high-quality RNA for downstream analysis.

parameters.sample_type: Physiologically matured leaves (V5 stage) from 7-year-old Persian lime trees
parameters.sample_conditions: naturally infected by HLB, with and without symptoms

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Total RNA was extracted from leaf tissue for RT-PCR and RT-qPCR validation

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Total RNA was extracted from leaf samples. [Gap] Need to remove genomic DNA contamination before cDNA synthesis. [Decision] To achieve DNA removal, chose to use RQ1 RNAse-free DNAse to treat RNA samples, expecting to obtain DNA-free RNA for accurate expression analysis.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: RNA samples were treated to remove genomic DNA contamination

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] RNA was extracted and DNAse-treated. [Gap] Need to verify RNA concentration and purity before cDNA synthesis. [Decision] To achieve RNA quality assessment, chose to use NanoDrop 1000 Spectrophotometer to examine concentration and purity, expecting to obtain quality metrics for RNA samples.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Concentration and purity of RNA samples were examined

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] RNA concentration and purity were measured. [Gap] Need to evaluate RNA integrity before proceeding to cDNA synthesis. [Decision] To achieve RNA quality evaluation, chose to use agarose gel electrophoresis to evaluate RNA quality, expecting to confirm RNA integrity for downstream applications.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: RNA quality was evaluated by agarose gel electrophoresis

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] High-quality RNA was confirmed from leaf samples. [Gap] Need to synthesize cDNA for PCR-based expression validation. [Decision] To achieve cDNA synthesis, chose to use M-MLV reverse transcriptase enzyme to synthesize single-strand cDNA, expecting to obtain cDNA for RT-PCR and RT-qPCR analysis.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Single-strand cDNA was synthesized from RNA samples

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] cDNA was synthesized from leaf RNA. [Gap] Need specific primers to evaluate ClCDR gene expression levels. [Decision] To achieve primer design, chose to use Primer Express Ver. 3.1 to design primers for ClCDR genes, expecting to obtain primer sets for RT-PCR and RT-qPCR amplification.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: A set of primers were designed to evaluate relative expression levels of ClCDR genes

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Primers were designed for ClCDR genes. [Gap] Need to perform initial gene expression evaluation by conventional PCR. [Decision] To achieve initial expression evaluation, chose to use Taq DNA polymerase to perform RT-PCR amplification, expecting to obtain amplification products for qualitative observation.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: No amplification was observed in almost all ClCDR genes, except ClCDR8a (TRINITY_DN3834_c0_g1 transcript)

references.relevance: SAND gene used as control for RT-PCR

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] RT-PCR amplification was performed on ClCDR genes. [Gap] Need to visualize and analyze RT-PCR products. [Decision] To achieve product visualization, chose to use agarose gel electrophoresis to analyze RT-PCR products, expecting to observe amplification patterns corresponding to transcriptome data.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Amplification pattern corresponded with transcriptome data counts, suggesting very low mRNA levels of ClCDR genes in samples

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] RT-PCR showed low ClCDR gene expression with only ClCDR8a detectable. [Gap] Need quantitative validation of ClCDR8a expression differences between symptomatic and asymptomatic samples. [Decision] To achieve quantitative validation, chose to use StepOne Real-Time PCR System with Sybr green master mix to perform RT-qPCR, expecting to obtain relative expression levels of ClCDR8a gene.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: No significant differences in ClCDR8a gene expression between HLB-symptomatic and asymptomatic Persian lime samples

references.relevance: UPL7 gene used as reference for normalization

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] RT-qPCR data was obtained for ClCDR8a expression in symptomatic and asymptomatic samples. [Gap] Need statistical analysis to determine significance of expression differences. [Decision] To achieve statistical validation, chose to use R Software to perform bilateral T-Student analysis on REL data, expecting to obtain statistical significance values for expression differences.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Statistical analysis showed no significant differences in ClCDR8a gene expression when CLas infection is established

log2_fold_change.interpretation: Low fold change indicates no significant differential expression of ClCDR genes during HLB infection

p_value.interpretation: Non-significant p-values indicate no statistically significant differences in ClCDR gene expression between conditions

relative_expression_levels.interpretation: RT-qPCR validation confirmed transcriptome data showing no significant expression differences in ClCDR8a between symptomatic and asymptomatic samples

sequence_similarity.interpretation: High similarity between ClCDR proteins and PtCDR/PtCDR homologs confirms successful identification of CDR gene family members

cdr_genes_identified.interpretation: Total sequences with aspartyl protease domain found; seven CDR clades recovered with at least six CDR family members in C. latifolia

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The research objective was partially achieved. CDR gene sequences were successfully identified in Persian lime transcriptome (146 sequences, 7 clades, at least 6 CDR family members). However, the hypothesis that HLB tolerance in Persian lime involves high expression of CDR genes similar to P. trifoliata was NOT supported. Both transcriptome data and RT-qPCR validation showed no significant differences in ClCDR gene expression between HLB-symptomatic and asymptomatic plants, suggesting that HLB tolerance in Persian lime is not correlated with increased expression of the CDR gene family.
